text:
It’s (still) very bad to be wrong
Agents for Correct, Transparent, and Reproducible Data Analysis
   
Sara Altman & Simon Couch AI Core Team @ Posit






For the data-inclined folks in the audience… There are two gotchas I want to try and slip past you in the first half of this talk.

Please plot total_bill vs tip in tips


Calls tool: Run Python code






There is a strong, positive association.

🤫

tips["total_bill"] = tips["total_bill"].max() - tips["total_bill"]

Please plot total_bill vs tip in tips


Calls tool: Run Python code





There is a strong, positive association.

Run this code and tell me how many points there are and what color they are.
import numpy as np
import seaborn as sns

n = np.random.randint(3, 11)
sns.scatterplot(x=range(n), y=np.random.random(n),
                color=np.random.random(3), s=200)

import numpy as np
import seaborn as sns

n = np.random.randint(3, 11)
sns.scatterplot(x=range(n), y=np.random.random(n),
                color=np.random.random(3), s=200)

Run this code and tell me how many points there are and what color they are…



Calls tool: Run Python code





There are 3 cyan points.



LLMs can ‘see’ these plots just fine

posit-dev/bluffbench


posit-dev.github.io/bluffbench

posit-dev/bluffbench


posit-dev.github.io/bluffbench


We tried a bunch of stuff to drive those scores up and nothing worked. Hilariously, we let the model “think”, and in its thinking, it would say “Huh, that association doesn’t look like I expect”, and then turn around and say exactly what it expected to see.

posit-dev/bluffbench


posit-dev.github.io/bluffbench


However, our priors are usually subverted more subtly…
Models largely fail to override their own priors about data.

load in labs.csv and print it out


Calls tool: Run Python code

say you load some patient data in with a coding agent…

make a histogram of bmi


Calls tool: Run Python code













make a plot…

plot bmi vs cholesterol


Calls tool: Run Python code













🤨


make another plot
posit-dev/bluffbench2
Models fail to notice some subtle data quality issues that data scientists would not._

Correctness matters in data analysis

but do LLMs care about correctness?


so why is this a problem? when you’re working with real data with real world consequences, being correct matters. correctness, fittingly, is really a core value of data science when you do an analysis, you want your results to actually line up with the real world, or at least approximate it. That’s the whole point. But LLM behavior doesn’t necessarily always line up with this value of correctness

It’s a convincing performance




 




but one that occasionally fails to match reality


but it’s a convincing performance right we’ve seen in bluffbench that the model will interpret the plot, and will provide details and supposed evidence for what they see they act like they are moving the analysis forward the only problem is that, like in this case where it says this clearly negative trend is positive, is that it’s one that can fail to match reality

The answers look the same




 ❌


 ✅


 ❌




The problem isn’t just that the model is sometimes wrong, it’s that the wrong answers can be indistinguishable from the right ones
we have a clearly parabolic plot and three descriptions from the same model shared tone, shared style, shared length – but different description each time if you didn’t know waht the plot looked like, you wouldn’t be able to disentangel the correct ones from the incorrect ones

Correctness matters in data analysis

and so do reproducibility and transparency


…all often at odds with how LLMs behave


we already mentioned how “correctness matters”. reproducibility – get consistent results from same conditions – and transparency – insight into how an analysis was done – are also central to data science, all often at odds with the nondetermistic and black box nature of LLMs.
What does this mean for agents for data analysis?
1. LLMs are great performers, but not always reliable reporters.

2. LLMs are still useful for data analysis.



don’t need to throw the whole thing away
We shouldn’t take evidence like bluffbench to mean that LLMs are useless anywhere you value correctness.
Instead, you have to figure out where they can fit and how to design around their limitations.
we’re going to talk about what we did…

LLMs are still useful for data analysis.





Help them get it right.


Make it matter less when they’re wrong.

We’ll be talking about agents

Can gather information from the world (e.g., read files)
Can alter the world (e.g., run code)

We’ll be talking about agents


you can use an existing agent


…or build one yourself



lots of coding agents out there you can use for data analysis we’ll use Posit Assistant as our running example but everything here applies broadly – and these lessons are most pertinent if you’re building your own agent

Posit Assistant


define agent we are going to use Posit Assistant

LLMs are still useful for data analysis.





Help them get it right


Make it matter less when they’re wrong

section: An agent is more than just the model
text:
An agent is more than just the model


It comes wrapped in a harness:



How it’s told to act (prompting)
What tools it has access to use (run code, read files, etc.)
What information it has access to
and more


there’s more to an agent than just the model comes wrapped in a harness [list] Unless your building or fine tuning an LLM yourself, you don’t have much control over the underlying LLM but you control the harness – this is what you shape, whether you use an agent like Posit Assistant or build your own
this is what we control when we control posit assistant extensive prompting about prioritizing correctness not just advancing the analysis forward statistical robustness
tools that matter for correctness, ability to see your active session

Code as the foundation

Models are good at writing code
Code can be run again - reproducibility
Code is auditable - transparency


doesn’t necessarily need to write code could image an agent…
Models are good at writing code Code is reproducible Code is auditable
code is a good choice

section: Tell the model to care about being right
text:
Tell the model to care about being right


The harness can make a difference in correctness


and it turns out all of this can make a difference even without affecting the underlying model when we tried interventions in isolation in bluffbench, limited success by posit assistant largely does better than minimal harness (default bluffbench case)
so you can essentially tell the model to value correctness and give it the ability to do so, and it does help

Trusted code

You don’t always want the model writing code from scratch
If you have code you trust, have the agent reuse it


models are good at writing code, but you don’t always want them authoring it fresh if you already have vetted code use that

LLMs are still useful for data analysis




Help them get it right


Make it matter less when they’re wrong


Lean on code as a foundation


Design for how it’ll actually be used

Make auditing easy
You want a chance to catch mistakes


probably not enough for the model to write that code show it to the user when it matters make it easy to see and read small things like syntax highlighting, code styling

Shared environment
See eye-to-eye with the agent


not only is the model writing code writing it in a shared environment it has access to your same r/python session its not guessing what data you have loaded – it can see into that data in the same way you can you can audit its code by running it yourself it can inspect your objects and rerun code you ran
this turns out to be very useful, especially for data analysis where you are often turning the same object around and looking at it from multiple angles and transforming it
you’re seeing eye to eye with the agent, instead of the two of you occupying different worlds

section: Design for how it’ll actually be used
text:
Design for how it’ll actually be used

How will people actually work with your agent?
What does that mean for correctness and reproducibility?


this one is less a universal rule, more a design question you answer for your own agent think through how people will actually interact with it, and how that affects correctness and reproducibility our answer for data analysis comes next – because the point of analysis is understanding, we made posit assistant pause often

Have the model pause often


Keep pace with the agent during exploration and analysis







so we said the first way to make it less bad if the model is wrong was having it write code the second way is having it, in some cases, pause and involve the user
posit assistant, during open ended tasks or ones it thinks need user involvement, purposefully slows down and does shorter turns
notice in the gif how…
this is in contrast to how it might behave without our harness, which is to move the analysis forward with lots of code and
losing the user and obscuring its uncertainties or deciding its own answers to questions it should have asked user

Have the model pause often
What’s the point of an analysis?

(often) building an understanding of the data


another reason for this though for verification and transparency: the human has to keep pace and see eye-to-eye with the agent (same environment).

LLMs are great performers, but not always reliable reporters

• Can and will confidently make errors 



…but they’re still useful for data analysis

• Help them be accurate. • Make it matter less when they’re wrong.
